data.title: Chubb Appoints Scott Henck Global Chief Actuary
data.description:
Cynthia Bentley promoted to succeed Henck as North America Chief Actuary; Paul O'Connell to Retire

ZURICH, Feb. 19, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — Chubb Limited today announced that Scott Henck, Executive Vice President and Chief Actuary, North America, has been appointed Senior Vice President, Chubb Group and Chief Actuary. He succeeds Paul O'Connell, who is retiring after a 40-year career in the property and casualty insurance industry. The appointment is effective April 1.

In his new role, Henck will oversee all actuarial functions, including reserving, pricing and capital performance measurement. He will report directly to Evan G. Greenberg, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Chubb Limited and Chubb Group and to Peter Enns, Executive Vice President, Chubb Group and Chief Financial Officer.

"On behalf of all of us at Chubb and me personally, I want to thank Paul for his many contributions, outstanding judgment and deep professionalism over his distinguished career," said Greenberg. "Paul has been an important steward of Chubb's actuarial capabilities, and we wish him all the best in his retirement."

"We are pleased to appoint Scott to lead Chubb's global actuarial functions. He is an accomplished actuary and proven leader whose deep technical expertise and understanding of our businesses will ensure we continue to manage risk with the rigor and excellence that define our company."

Enns added, "Scott's analytical acumen and collaborative leadership style make him the ideal successor to further drive the excellence of our global actuarial operations."

Cynthia Bentley, currently Senior Vice President and Head Actuary of North America Commercial Insurance, will succeed Henck as Executive Vice President, North America Chief Actuary. She will report to Henck and Juan Luis Ortega, Executive Vice President, Chubb Group and President, North America Insurance, who added, "Cynthia's appointment is a reflection of her outstanding track record and the tremendous impact she has had on our company. I'm confident she will continue to drive innovation and further strengthen our actuarial capabilities across our North America businesses."

Biographies

Scott brings to the role nearly three decades of insurance industry experience. He joined Chubb in 2002 and has most recently served as Chief Actuary of North America from 2019. Prior to that role, he founded and led the Actuarial Insights, Business Intelligence, and Advanced Analytics unit for Global Claims. Scott is a Fellow of the Casualty Actuarial Society (FCAS), a Member of the American Academy of Actuaries (MAAA), a Chartered Property Casualty Underwriter (CPCU), and a graduate of Lebanon Valley College.

Cynthia has more than three decades of insurance and actuarial experience. Prior to serving as Head Actuary of North America, she led the Major Accounts Actuarial support for Property & Specialty and Financial Lines. Cynthia is a graduate of Bucknell University and a Fellow of the Casualty Actuarial Society.

About Chubb

Chubb is a world leader in insurance. With operations in 54 countries and territories, Chubb provides commercial and personal property and casualty insurance, personal accident and supplemental health insurance, reinsurance and life insurance to a diverse group of clients. The company is defined by its extensive product and service offerings, broad distribution capabilities, exceptional financial strength and local operations globally. Parent company Chubb Limited is listed on the New York Stock Exchange and is a component of the S&P 500 index. Chubb employs approximately 45,000 people worldwide. Additional information can be found at: www.chubb.com.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/chubb-appoints-scott-henck-global-chief-actuary-302692962.html

SOURCE Chubb

data.title: Corcept Loses Patent-Infringement Appeal Against Teva
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Corcept Therapeutics lost its patent-infringement battle with Teva Pharmaceutical Industries, exposing Corcept to more competition from generic drugs.

The ruling from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit upheld a previous finding against Corcept's claims that Teva was infringing on its patent for the drug Korlym.

In 2018, Corcept first sued Teva over its application to market a generic version of Korlym, which is approved to treat people with Cushing's syndrome. A judge found in favor of Teva in 2023, and Corcept appealed the ruling.

Corcept's stock fell 18%, to $32.60, on Thursday, at one point touching a 52-week low of $28.66. Shares have lost half their value over the past 12 months.

Teva has counter-sued, alleging Corcept is monopolizing the market for Korlym.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Amazon can be sued over suicides linked to sodium nitrite, court rules
data.description:
The Washington Supreme Court ruled unanimously on Thursday that Amazon.com must face lawsuits brought by families with relatives who took their own lives by consuming sodium nitrite they bought on the online retailer's platform.

It rejected a lower court's ruling that the families could not pursue negligence claims under a Washington state product liability law, because suicide was a superseding cause of their relatives' deaths.

Four families accused Amazon of promoting the sale of sodium nitrite on its website alongside other products that could assist people in carrying out suicides.

They said the Seattle-based retailer has known of the link between sodium nitrite and suicide for years yet continued to sell the product without restrictions.

Amazon and its lawyers did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

data.title: West Virginia Attorney General Files Lawsuit Alleging Apple's iCloud Allowed for Distribution of Child Pornography
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

JB McCuskey, the attorney general of West Virginia, filed a lawsuit against Apple, alleging the company allowed its iCloud platform to be used to store and distribute child sexual abuse material.

The lawsuit, filed Thursday, alleges that Apple's iCloud platform, which is designed to make images and videos easier to locate and share across devices and applications, reduces friction for those looking to maintain large collections of illicit material and enables repeated access and redistribution without manual file handling.

Federal law requires all U.S. technology companies to report detected child sexual abuse material to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children. In 2023, Apple made 267 such reports, while Alphabet's Google filed about 1.5 million reports and Meta Platforms filed more than 30.6 million, the complaint said.

"Rather than implement industry-standard detection tools used by its peers, Apple repeatedly shirked their responsibility to protect children under the guise of user privacy," the West Virginia Attorney General's Office said.

The lawsuit alleges Apple can't claim to be an unknowing, passive conduit of child sexual abuse material, given it maintains end-to-end control over its hardware, software and cloud infrastructure.

Apple said in a statement that protecting the safety and privacy of its users, especially children, is key to its mission. The company pointed to its parental controls and features such as communication safety, which is designed to automatically intervene on kids' devices when nudity is detected in messages, shared photos, AirDrop and live FaceTime calls. The feature is turned on by default for children under 18.

"We are innovating every day to combat ever-evolving threats and maintain the safest, most trusted platform for kids," Apple said.

The suit seeks statutory and punitive damages as well as injunctive relief requiring Apple to implement effective detection measures for child sexual abuse material. The lawsuit also seeks remedies mandating safer product design going forward.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: BioNTech sues Moderna for patent infringement over COVID shots
data.description:
German biopharmaceutical company BioNTech sued Moderna in Delaware federal court on Thursday, alleging that Moderna's COVID-19 vaccine mNEXSPIKE infringes a patent related to BioNTech and Pfizer's competing shot Comirnaty.

The lawsuit said that mNEXSPIKE, Moderna's next-generation COVID shot approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration in 2025, violates BioNTech's rights in technology for a streamlined messenger RNA-based vaccine design that can be given to patients at a lower dosage.

Moderna sued BioNTech and its partner Pfizer for patent infringement over Comirnaty in 2022, in a lawsuit that is ongoing. The cases are among a wave of patent lawsuits from biotech companies seeking royalties for the technology used in the blockbuster vaccines.

Spokespeople for Moderna and BioNTech did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the new lawsuit. Spokespeople for Pfizer, which is not involved in the lawsuit, also did not immediately comment.

data.title: Klarna swings to loss, shares tumble
data.description:
In the year to the end of December, the company swung to a net loss of $273m from a profit of $21m the year before, while total revenue rose to $3.5bn from $2.8bn.

In the fourth quarter, the Stockholm-based group swung to a net loss of $26m from a profit of $40m in the same period a year earlier, while total revenue was 38% higher at $1.1bn. This was the company’s first billion-dollar quarter and revenue was above guidance, but analysts had been expecting a Q4 loss closer to $10m.

Klarna said its consumer base expanded to 118m active consumers in the fourth quarter, up 28% year-over-year, while the number of merchants using it grew 42% to 966,000.

The company’s shares sank 26% on the results.

Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell, said: "It’s been a tough day for ‘buy now, pay later’ services provider Klarna. Its shares have dropped sharply after it reported a wider than expected loss despite rising revenue.

"Today’s fall takes the drop in share price since its September IPO to almost 70%, challenging company bosses to maintain investor confidence. Whilst promises of jam tomorrow are frustrating for those who’d rather enjoy their jam today, the company is growing rapidly and navigating away from the short-term loans to cover retail purchases that it is best known for.

"Making use of AI has been at the forefront of the company’s cost cutting efforts and it is adding customers for products like its debit card and more traditional loans. But with consumers still under pressure from the uncomfortable cost of living, there will be concern about the ability of some people to keep up their payments, as well as the impact from increased regulation on the sector."

data.title: Boehringer Ingelheim Gets FDA Emergency Use Authorization for Screwworm Treatments
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

The Food and Drug Administration granted Boehringer Ingelheim emergency use authorizations for its treatments of New World screwworm in dogs and cats.

New World screwworm is a parasitic fly that lays its eggs in open wounds or soft tissue of warm‑blooded animals. The larvae then burrow into the flesh, causing damage that can be fatal.

New World screwworm isn't currently present in the U.S., but there have been recent cases in northern Mexico, Boehringer Ingelheim said.

"Screwworm infestations can escalate quickly, and treatment delays put animals at real risk," said Steve Maschmedt, Boehringer Ingelheim's executive director of pet veterinary technical services. "Having authorized treatment options available enables veterinarians to respond quickly when NWS infestations are suspected."

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: EU wheat rallies as US weather and euro support
data.description:
Euronext wheat rose on Thursday to a 2-1/2 month high as weather concerns fuelled a rally in Chicago while an easing in the euro boosted European export prospects, traders said.

May milling wheat (BL2K6), the most active position on Euronext, settled 2% up at 195 euros ($229.50) a metric ton, after touching its highest since November 26 at 195.25 euros.

Chicago wheat also added around 2%, having hit its highest since early July.

High winds in the U.S. Plains, which have fanned wildfires and raised worries about soil erosion in some dry zones, have added impetus to short-covering in the wheat market.

"Chicago and Kansas wheat climbed to multi-month highs on strong U.S. export sales, fund short-covering and Southern Plains weather risks, including drought and wind damage," British merchant ADM Agriculture said.

Euronext, which had lagged gains in Chicago on Wednesday, drew momentum on Thursday from a lower euro .

"Markets are currently very currency-driven and the weaker euro is bringing French wheat back into contention in the important Egyptian market," a German trader said.

Russian, French and Ukrainian 11.5% protein wheat for March/April shipment to Egypt were all quoted around $249-$250 a ton cost and freight (c&f) included to Egypt, he said, with Romanian about $3 higher.

Some demand was seen this week from buyers in north and west Africa, but cheaper prices from rival Argentine and Black Sea suppliers along with lack of farmer selling made it difficult to sell German wheat, traders said.

There was uncertainty over demand prospects in Morocco, the European Union's biggest wheat export market, where heavy rain has raised the prospect of a bigger local crop this year while also delaying unloading of recent shipments.

In France, traders were monitoring incessant rain that has caused flooding in some western areas, though the threat to wheat crops was seen as limited for now.

Inconclusive peace negotiations between Russia and Ukraine, and tensions between Iran and the United States also helped underpin wheat like other commodity markets, traders said.

($1 = 0.8497 euros)

data.title: Pokemon Co. International to Acquire Excell Brands
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Pokemon Company International agreed to acquire Excell Brands, a U.S.-based trading cards distributor and category management service provider.

The two companies have had a decades-long relationship, and Excell has become Pokemon's largest distribution partner in the U.S., the company said Thursday.

It did not provide financial details of the acquisition.

The company said it would invest in Excell's capabilities while maintaining its independence with retail and brand partners.

"This reflects our long-term commitment to supporting the Pokémon community and the broader trading card ecosystem," Pokemon Company International President Kenji Okubo said. "Excell Brands has built a strong reputation as a trusted distributor and category management partner for their customers with deep insight into the needs of Pokémon fans."

In recent years, Pokemon's trading cards have skyrocketed in value. Between 2004 and August 2025, Pokemon cards saw a roughly 3,821% cumulative return, The Wall Street Journal reported, eclipsing the S&P 500's returns over the same period.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Klarna's Quarterly Revenue Tops $1 Billion. Wall Street Isn't Happy. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Klarna, the Swedish buy now, pay later provider, posted what it called its "first billion-dollar revenue quarter" on Thursday. The stock reaction indicates investors weren't all that impressed.

Revenue of $1.08 billion edged past the $1.07 billion Wall Street had forecast. Gross merchandise volume, a measure of the total value of all the products sold through Klarna's network in a specific period, came to $38.7 billion, narrowly beating out analysts' calls for $38.1 billion.

The fintech reported 118 million active users as of the fourth quarter, better than the 117 million Wall Street was anticipating. However, average revenue per active consumer was flat at $30.

Klarna also posted a $241 million pretax loss for the period, marking the second quarterly loss in a row since its initial public offering.

Shares sank 25% to $14.13, putting Klarna on pace for its largest single-day percent decrease on record as well as its lowest-ever closing level. Fellow BNPL provider Affirm was down 0.5%.

A conservative first-quarter outlook could have something to do with it. For the current quarter, Klarna sees revenue in the range of $900 million to $980 million. Analysts were looking for $965.1 million, well above the midpoint of that range. The BNPL provider is also targeting gross merchandise volume of $32 billion to $33 billion, compared with expectations for $33.4 billion.

A closer look at the earnings reports showed that results were largely mixed. J.P. Morgan analysts pointed out that revenue and GMV topped expectations but adjusted operating income and transaction margin dollars were below Wall Street forecasts.

Klarna reported $372 million in transaction margin income, below calls for $395 million. This marks the second consecutive quarter the metric missed analysts' estimates. Meanwhile, adjusted operating margin came to 4.4%, sharply below forecasts of 6.4%.

Management attributed the shortfall in transaction margin income to the faster-than-expected scaling of "expanded banking products," likely its installment-based Fair Financing offering. J.P. Morgan said the number fell short of what its financial model had forecast as a result of processing and funding costs.

For 2026, Klarna told investors to expect GMV of more than $155 billion, while the Wall Street consensus view was for $159 billion. The company also expects revenue to rise more than 24%. J.P. Morgan and Wall Street, respectively, were looking for increases of 31% and 29%.

The full-year outlook "missed Street estimates across most all key metrics," analysts wrote. "The company is growing fast, and evidently the transition from scaling to engagement and lending growth is weighing on some KPIs," or key performance indicators.

KBW analysts echoed the sentiment. "While revenues beat on the quarter on better gain on sale and transaction revenue relative to our expectations, TMD was below our expectations" as well as the company's own guidance, the firm wrote, referring to transaction margin dollars.

Investors also might be waiting for the company to turn a quarterly net profit for the first time since its IPO. Adjusted operating expenses climbed 18% higher to $352 million in the latest quarter.

Klarna made its public trading debut in September, when its shares were listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Shares ended the first session up 15% from the IPO price of $40, though this momentum quickly fizzled.

Heading into the results, shares had fallen more than 34% this year, in line with a broader decline in financial technology stocks. Klarna and its peers within the broader fintech ecosystem, not just BNPL providers, have been caught up in concerns over how artificial intelligence might disrupt their business.

At the same time, a proposed 10% cap on credit-card interest rates has weighed on the shares since January, despite some firms like Mizuho Securities insisting the move would be a boon to BNPL providers. The logic behind that is that if rates are capped, banks might limit lower-income borrowers' access to credit, giving them a reason to use BNPL services.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Gemini stock slump may already factor in leadership exits, Mizuho says
data.description:
Shares of Gemini Space Station (GEMI) remain near all-time lows, but Mizuho analysts argue much of the recent turmoil may already be priced in.

In a Wednesday note, Dan Dolev and Alexander Jenkins reiterated their "outperform" rating and $26 price target, writing that while the departure of Gemini's COO, CFO, and CLO is "disappointing," the stock "likely already reflects a lot of this pain."

Gemini is trading at around $5.90 on Thursday, according to The Block price data, down a massive 43% in just the past 30 days alone and well below Mizuho’s base-case valuation.

Revenue slightly ahead, losses wider

Gemini’s preliminary revenue range of $165 million to $175 million came in slightly above Mizuho’s $168 million midpoint estimate. Adjusted EBITDA was weaker than expected, however, at roughly negative $257 million to negative $267 million versus the firm's prior forecast of about negative $224 million.

Still, the analysts argue recently announced cost reductions, including a roughly 25% workforce cut and exits from the UK, EU, and Australia, should help "drive a path to profitability over time."

They also described Gemini as a "crypto-heavy app," suggesting some of the weakness reflects broader pressure across digital-asset markets rather than company-specific factors alone.

Valuation unchanged

Mizuho continues to value Gemini at 7x estimated 2027 revenue, a discount to the 10x peer group median, supporting its unchanged $26 price target.

In a more optimistic scenario that includes stronger monthly transacting user growth and faster margin recovery, the firm sees shares trading closer to peer multiples, implying a $43 bull-case target. From current levels, that would represent gains of more than 600%.

By contrast, Mizuho’s bear case assumes slower user growth and a tougher operating backdrop, implying an $8 target — a 35% jump from where the stock trades today.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Slashes Fees for Private-Market Investments Through EquityZen — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Morgan Stanley is cutting fees for investments in private companies by half, inviting wealthy clients to expand their positions in businesses that don't trade on public exchanges. The move follows Morgan Stanley's Jan. 27 close of the acquisition of EquityZen, a platform for trading private shares.

Under the new rate, which was announced Thursday and takes effect immediately, Morgan Stanley clients will pay a 2.5% fee on most purchases and sales of private-company shares on the EquityZen platform, down from 5%. The minimum investment for a private-market transaction is holding steady at $5,000.

Clients who want to take advantage of private-market trading on the EquityZen platform must qualify as accredited investors, a regulatory stipulation that limits access to many private investments to individuals with a minimum net worth or annual income, or those who hold certain professional licenses.

There have long been calls to expand the accredited-investor definition. Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Paul Atkins has said he is considering updating the rule to expand access to more investors, and there is strong bipartisan support in Congress for modernizing the definition, including a bill approved by the House that would consider an investor's professional experience and establish a test to determine eligibility for accredited status.

"Bringing down fees allows more clients to transact and build wealth in a frictionless, holistic way," says Jed Finn, head of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management. The EquityZen acquisition "was about bringing a comprehensive end-to-end solution to our clients," he notes. As more companies stay on the sidelines of public markets for longer periods, clients increasingly have been looking for ways to access those investment opportunities.

EquityZen says its mission is to bring "private markets to the public." It has partnerships with nearly 500 private companies to offer access to their shares, which Finn has said was a crucial element of EquityZen's appeal — that it works with issuers to ensure they retain control over the movement and pricing of their shares.

The EquityZen acquisition also connects to another of Morgan Stanley's strategic priorities — to expand its partnership with young companies in various stages of their journey toward a potential public offering.

Morgan Stanley has a partnership with Carta, a leading provider of capitalization tables that help early-stage companies keep track of their equity ownership, which pairs with its own cap-table offering for companies closer to an IPO.

In both cases, Morgan Stanley sees deeper engagement with founders and employees of private companies as a potentially lucrative pipeline for its advisory business as those individuals accumulate wealth and seek financial guidance.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: PureTech Health Gets Orphan Drug Designation for Deupirfenidone Lung Treatment
data.description:
By Chris Wack

PureTech Health said the Food and Drug Administration and European Commission have granted orphan drug designation to deupirfenidone LYT-100 for the treatment of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, a rare, progressive and fatal lung disease.

The biotherapeutics company said that deupirfenidone is being advanced by Celea Therapeutics, an entity established by PureTech to lead its late stage development and potential commercialization.

Orphan drug designation is intended to support the development of therapies for rare diseases, defined as conditions affecting fewer than 200,000 people in the U.S. or fewer than five in 10,000 individuals in the European Union.

PureTech said Celea Therapeutics intends to finalize financing in the first half of 2026 to support the initiation of a Phase 3 trial in the first half of 2026. The trial will compare deupirfenidone 825 mg to pirfenidone 801 mg in a head-to-head study powered to test for superiority.

Based on feedback from the FDA and other global health authorities, PureTech believes that the results from this single Phase 3 trial could complete the data package required to support potential registration of deupirfenidone.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com
